context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m trying to do the City Case Study Spider Diagram for my climate action course, and I’m a little lost on how to organize it. Could you help me figure out what kind of information I’m supposed to put for each of the 5 pathways?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—but let’s organize it, not fill it in for you. First, for each of the 5 pathways, you’re usually looking for: 1) key climate problems in the city, 2) actions/policies/projects, 3) who is involved in decisions, and 4) any evidence of progress or gaps.

A good starting question: what are the 5 pathway names in your course materials, and which city did you choose? Once you list those, we can sort what kind of evidence belongs under each one.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will stay in the tutor role and avoid filling in the assignment for the student. I’ll use guided discovery by helping the student identify the structure of the Spider Diagram and prompting them to connect course concepts to their chosen city. I will keep the response brief, focused on the assignment, and ask a forward-moving Socratic question so the student does the thinking about what belongs in each pathway.

exchanges.student: I picked Medellín, but I’m not totally sure I remember the exact 5 pathway names from the course. I think they’re about things like infrastructure/governance maybe, but I’m mixing them up. Could you help me identify the pathway names first so I can stop sorting info into the wrong buckets?
exchanges.tutor:
Don’t guess here—wrong buckets will hurt the whole diagram. Open the course page or assignment handout and find the exact list of the 5 pathways for urban transformation.

When you have them, paste just the 5 names here. Then we’ll do the useful part: for each pathway, we’ll decide what kind of Medellín evidence belongs there and what does not.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not supply uncertain course-specific content from memory as if definitive. Instead, I’ll guide the student to verify the exact pathway names from their course materials, since accuracy matters for the assignment. I’ll keep it bite-sized, use Socratic prompting, and help them build a usable organizing method without writing the assignment for them.

exchanges.student: Okay, I checked. The 5 pathways are: urban form, urban infrastructure, governance, innovation, and behavior. I’m still not confident about the difference between urban form and urban infrastructure for Medellín though.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now separate shape from systems.

Ask yourself: is this about how the city is physically arranged, or about the services/networks that run through it?
- Urban form = layout/pattern of land use, density, where housing/jobs are, connectivity of neighborhoods.
- Urban infrastructure = transport, energy, water, waste, drainage, public works systems.

Try this: give me 2 Medellín examples you’ve found, and I’ll challenge whether each fits form or infrastructure.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep the student focused on the assignment and use Socratic questioning to help them distinguish two commonly confused categories without supplying Medellín-specific content or direct answers. I’ll give minimal conceptual contrast and then ask the student to test it with examples, moving them toward organizing their own spider diagram.

exchanges.student: I found the Metrocable system and also the green corridors project. My first guess is Metrocable = infrastructure, but green corridors could be form? I’m not sure because it’s physical space too.
exchanges.tutor:
Your first instinct is plausible, but don’t stop at labels. Use a test:

What is each project mainly changing?
- movement/service delivery?
- spatial pattern/land use/public space?

For green corridors, ask: are they mainly an environmental/public-space design shaping the city’s layout and heat exposure, or mainly a service network like transport/water/energy?

Pick one category for each and defend it in one sentence each.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student's productive reasoning without confirming a final classification outright. I’ll use guided discovery by giving a criterion-based test they can apply themselves, and ask them to justify the categorization in their own words. This keeps the response brief, on-task, and avoids directly supplying the answer.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’d put Metrocable under urban infrastructure because it changes the transport network and improves mobility/access. For green corridors, I’d put it under urban form because it changes public space and the physical design of streets, even though it also has climate benefits.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a defensible distinction. Good.

Now push beyond naming projects. For each one, what 3 pieces of information can you add to make the spider diagram stronger?
Think: climate purpose, main actors, and evidence of impact or limitation.

Try that for just Metrocable first.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will reinforce the student's reasoning and push them one step further toward assignment-ready organization by asking them to identify the specific dimensions they should note for each pathway entry. I will avoid writing for them and keep the response concise and forward-moving.

exchanges.student: For Metrocable, I think the climate purpose could be lowering car use or connecting hillside communities to transit so emissions might go down. Main actors would probably be the city government and the transit agency, maybe Metro de Medellín? Evidence of impact/limitation is where I get stuck because I’m not sure if I need actual data or if a general effect is enough.
exchanges.tutor:
Don’t stay vague if you can avoid it. For this assignment, concrete evidence is stronger than a general effect.

Ask: can you find any of these for Metrocable?
- a reported outcome
- a stated goal from the city/transit agency
- a credible claim about access, emissions, or social inclusion

Even one specific, sourced point is better than a broad guess. What source have you looked at so far?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will address the student’s uncertainty by clarifying the level of evidence likely appropriate for the assignment without overcommitting to requirements I cannot verify. I’ll guide them to prioritize credible, concrete evidence and ask a specific question that helps them decide what kind of support they can realistically include. I will not provide wording for the submission.

exchanges.student: Honestly I’ve mostly just looked at general webpages and I’m kind of skimming. I’m not sure which sources count as credible enough for this class. Could you tell me what kinds of sources I should prioritize so I’m not wasting time?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes. Prioritize in this order:
1) official city/government plans or agency pages
2) reports from recognized international organizations or research institutes
3) peer-reviewed or university sources
4) reputable news, only for context

Use general webpages last, not first. For Medellín, what’s one official source you could search right now—for example, city government, Metro agency, or climate plan?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will provide process guidance about source credibility, which is appropriate tutoring support, while still requiring the student to make choices. I’ll keep it concise, prioritize source types relevant to the assignment, and ask the student to apply the hierarchy to Medellín so they stay engaged in the work.

exchanges.student: I could search the Medellín city government site, Metro de Medellín, or maybe a city climate action plan if they have one. I’m a little unsure how to turn that into the spider diagram table though—like do I need full sentences or just short evidence notes under each pathway?
exchanges.tutor:
For the table option, aim for short evidence notes, not polished paragraphs. Keep each pathway entry compact: project/policy, climate relevance, actors, and one evidence point or gap.

So: brief phrases are usually better than full sentences if they’re clear and specific.

Try drafting one row for urban infrastructure in note form only. What would you put?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will answer the format question by steering the student toward concise, evidence-based notes rather than polished writing, which matches the assignment’s table option and avoids writing for them. I’ll use a focused question to get them to draft one entry themselves.

exchanges.student:
Maybe something like:

Urban infrastructure:
- Project/policy: Metrocable integrated transit system
- Climate relevance: supports lower-emission mobility / reduces car dependence
- Actors: Metro de Medellín, city government
- Evidence/gap: improved access for hillside communities; need a more specific source or data on emissions impact

Is that the right level of detail, or is that still too vague?
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the right level of detail structurally. The weak spot is exactly what you noticed: evidence.

Make it less vague by asking: what kind of source would verify “improved access”—an agency report, city plan, or study? And can you name the limitation more precisely than “need more data”?

Revise just that last bullet.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the student's progress while pushing for sharper specificity without rewriting their work. I’ll use a brief evaluative comment and a forward-moving Socratic prompt to help them strengthen the weakest element: evidence/source precision.

exchanges.student:
Okay, maybe:

- Evidence/gap: Metro de Medellín or city mobility/climate documents may verify improved access/connectivity for informal hillside neighborhoods; gap = I haven’t found a specific measured emissions reduction from Metrocable yet.

I think that’s more precise, but I’m not sure if it sounds too much like a note to myself instead of part of the assignment.
exchanges.tutor:
Better, but yes—the first half still sounds like a note to yourself. The diagram should show what you found, not what you plan to find.

Ask yourself: what can you state now as a supported claim, and what should be labeled as a gap in available evidence? Split those cleanly.

Now do the same for green corridors under urban form. If you want, draft it and I’ll test whether it fits the pathway.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep the student focused on the assignment format and avoid editing their wording. I’ll confirm the conceptual direction, point out the remaining issue—distinguishing assignment content from a research-process note—and ask them to convert it into submission-ready evidence categories on their own. This maintains Socratic guidance and avoids writing for them.
